question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 55/S/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 27 printed pages.
  2. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

PHYSICS (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five sections - Sections {tex}\boldsymbol{A}, \boldsymbol{B}, \boldsymbol{C}, \boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  3. In Section A: Question numbers 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. In Section B: Question numbers 17 to 21 are Very Short Answer type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. In Section C: Question numbers 22 to 28 are Short Answer type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. In Section D: Question numbers 29 & 30 are case study-based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. In Section E: Question numbers 31 to 33 are Long Answer type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the Sections except Section A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculators is not allowed.
    You may use the following values of physical constants wherever necessary:
    {tex} c=3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s} {/tex}
    {tex} {h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} {Js} {/tex}
    {tex} {e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C} {/tex}
    {tex} \mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {~T} {~m} {~A}^{-1} {/tex}
    {tex} \varepsilon_0=8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 {~N}^{-1} {~m}^{-2} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-2} {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Mass of electron }\left({m}_{{e}}\right)=9.1 \times 10^{-31} {~kg} . {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Mass of neutron }=1.675 \times 10^{-27} {~kg} . {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Mass of proton }=1.673 \times 10^{-27} {~kg} . {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Avogadro's number }=6.023 \times 10^{23} {per} \text { gram mole } {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Boltzman's constant }=1.38 \times 10^{-23} {JK}^{-1} {/tex}

Section - A
1.

A point charge of 20 nC is fixed at the origin of {tex}x-y{/tex} plane. The work done in taking a second point charge of -50 nC from point ({tex}4 \mathrm{~cm}, 0{/tex}) to point {tex}(0,5 \mathrm{~cm}){/tex} is:

a. {tex}45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
b. {tex}-45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
c. {tex}90 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
d. {tex}-90 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
1
2.

In Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, the kinetic energy of an electron in {tex}{n}=2{/tex} orbit is:

a. 1.7 eV
b. {tex}3.4 {eV}{/tex}
c. 6.8 eV
d. {tex}10.2 {eV}{/tex}
1
3.

A galvanometer having a coil resistance of {tex}48 \Omega{/tex} shows full-scale deflection for a current of 2.0 A. It can be converted into an ammeter of range ({tex}0-10 {~A}{/tex}) by connecting:

a. in series a resistance of {tex}8 \Omega{/tex}
b. in parallel a resistance of {tex}8 \Omega{/tex}
c. in parallel a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex}
d. in series a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex}
1
4.

A charge Q is enclosed by a Gaussian spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is doubled, then the outward electric flux will

a. increase four times
b. be reduced to half
c. remain the same
d. be doubled
1
5.

A rectangular loop of area vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{A}}=[\hat{i}+\sqrt{3} \hat{j}] \times 10^{-2} {~m}^{2}{/tex} is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}=\left[4 .0 {t}^{2}+2 .0\right] \hat{{i}}{/tex}, where B is in tesla and t in seconds. At {tex}{t}=1 .0 {~s}{/tex}, the magnitude of induced emf in the loop is:

a. 80 mV
b. {tex}0 .11 {~V}{/tex}
c. 0.22 V
d. 0.8 V
1
6.

Which of the following electromagnetic waves are known as 'heat waves'?

a. UV rays
b. IR rays
c. X-rays
d. Gamma rays
1
7.

Which of the following assumptions has been used to obtain the relation {tex}\frac{v_{s}}{v_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} for a transformer?

a. The resistance of the primary coil is large.
b. The same flux links both the primary and the secondary coils.
c. The resistance of secondary coil is large.
d. The transformer is {tex}100 \%{/tex} efficient.
1
8.

The figure shows the variation of maximum kinetic energy ({tex}{K}_{\max }{/tex}) of emitted electrons as a function of the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of radiation incident on a photosensitive surface. The slope of the curve is:

a. h
b. {tex}\frac{{h}}{v}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex}
d. he
1
9.

The electric field due to an infinitely long, straight, uniformly and positively charged wire at a point at distance {tex}r{/tex} from the wire is:

a. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed outward
b. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} and is directed outward
c. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed inward
d. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} and is directed inward
1
10.

The potential energy of a pair of nucleons is minimum when they are separated by about:

a. {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-15} {~m}{/tex}
b. {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-10} {~m}{/tex}
c. {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-13} {~m}{/tex}
d. {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex}
1
11.

Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry the same current and attract each other with a force F per unit length. If the current is doubled in each conductor and their separation is made half, the force per unit length becomes:

a. {tex}\frac{{F}}{2}{/tex}
b. 2 F
c. 4 F
d. 8 F
1
12.

Whenever a magnet is moved either towards or away from a conducting coil, an emf is induced, the magnitude of which is independent of:

a. the strength of the magnetic field
b. the speed with which the magnet is moved
c. the number of turns in the coil
d. the resistance of the coil
1

Questions number 13 to 16 are Assertion (A) and Reason (R) type questions. Two statements are given - one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.
13.

Assertion (A): In process of photoelectric emission using a monochromatic light, all emitted electrons do not have the same kinetic energy.
Reason (R): If radiation falling on a photosensitive surface consists of different wavelengths, the energy of emitted photoelectrons by absorbing photons of different wavelengths, shall be different.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
14.

Assertion (A): The electrical conductivity of a pure Ge crystal increases with increase in its temperature.
Reason (R): The number of electrons excited by thermal excitation from the valence band to the conduction band, in a semiconductor, increases with increase in temperature.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
15.

Assertion (A): Electrostatic force is a conservative force.
Reason (R): In an electrostatic field, the work done between two points per unit positive charge depends on the path followed.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
16.

Assertion (A): The image of an object placed between {tex}f{/tex} and {tex}2 f{/tex} from a convex lens can be seen on a screen. If the screen is removed, image is not formed.
Reason (R): Rays from a given point on the object placed between f and 2f, after passing through a convex lens, do not converge on a point in space.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. Both A and R are false.
1

Section - B
17.

An electron of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}e{/tex} is accelerated from rest through a potential V. Prove that, after acceleration, the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{~V}}}{/tex}.

 2
18.

With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the method to obtain {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction diode characteristic when it is forward biased. Draw the shape of the characteristic.

 2
19.(i)

A plano-convex lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is placed coaxially in contact with a biconcave lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} as shown in the figure. All curved faces are of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} each. Obtain the expression for the focal length of the combined lens.

 2

OR
(ii)

A convex lens is kept coaxially on the left side of a concave mirror at a distance of 5f from it where f is focal length of each of them. An object is kept at a distance of 2f on the left side of the convex lens. Draw the ray diagram showing the formation of the image by the combination. Find the distance of the final image from the mirror.

 2
20.

The figure shows a network of resistors connected across a battery. Find the current supplied by the battery of emf 5 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac{2}{3} \Omega{/tex}.

 2
21.

In a Young's double-slit experiment with light of wavelength 520 nm, the fringe width is 0.52 mm. What will be the fringe width if the entire apparatus is immersed in water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex})?

 2

Section - C
22.

Define electric field. Write its SI unit. A point charge {tex}q=25 \mu {C}{/tex} is located at the point (0, 0) in x-y plane. Find (i) the magnitude of electric field at point ({tex}3 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}{/tex}), and (ii) the angle the field makes with the x-axis.

 3
23.(i)

A {tex}20 \mu {~F}{/tex} capacitor is connected to a {tex}220 {~V}, \frac{125}{\pi} {~Hz}{/tex} ac source.

  1. Find the capacitive reactance and the peak value of current in the circuit.
  2. If the frequency of the ac source is doubled, by what factor will the capacitive reactance and the current be changed?
 3

OR
(ii)

An electron (mass {tex}9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}{/tex} and charge {tex}1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}{/tex}) is moving in a circle with a speed of {tex}3.2 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}{/tex} in a magnetic field of {tex}4.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}{/tex} perpendicular to it.
Calculate:

  1. the radius of the circular path,
  2. its frequency of rotation, and
  3. its energy.
 3
24.

Define binding energy (BE) of a nucleus. An unstable nucleus has mass number {tex}{A}=240{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{BE}}{{A}}=7 \cdot 6 {MeV}{/tex}. It splits in two fragments, each of {tex}{A}=120{/tex} with {tex}\frac{{BE}}{{A}}=8.5 {MeV}{/tex}. Calculate the energy released.

 3
25.

Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity?
A point charge q is at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d, as shown in the figure. Use Gauss’ law to obtain the expression for the electric flux through the square.

If the point charge is now moved to a distance ‘d’ from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux will be affected.

 3
26.

What is thermonuclear fusion?
Calculate the energy released in MeV in the fusion reaction given below:
{tex}{ }_{1}^{2} {H}+{ }_{1}^{3} {H} \longrightarrow{ }_{2}^{4} {He}+{n}{/tex}
Given: {tex}{m}\left({ }_{1}^{2} {H}\right)=2 \cdot 014102 {u}, {m}\left({ }_{1}^{3} {H}\right){/tex}{tex}=3 \cdot 016049 {u}, {m}\left({ }_{2}^{4} {He}\right)=4 \cdot 002603 {u}{/tex},
{tex}{m}_{{n}}=1 \cdot 008665 {u}, 1 {u}=930 {MeV} / {c}^{2}{/tex}

 3
27.

Two cells are connected in parallel by connecting their positive terminals together and connecting their negative terminals together. Derive expression for the equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination.

 3
28.

A rectangular loop ({tex}1 {~cm} \times 5 {~cm}{/tex}) of wire carrying current of 0.4 A is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}=(0.03 \hat{i}+0.04 \hat{k}) {T}{/tex} such that the outward unit vector normal to the plane of the loop is {tex}(0.60 \hat{i}-0.80 \hat{j}){/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop, and
  2. the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the loop.
 3

Section - D

Questions number 29 and 30 are case study-based questions. Read the following paragraphs and answer the questions that follow.
29.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
According to the band theory of solids, a semiconductor has a valence band and a conduction band separated by a gap, known as energy band gap. Pure semiconductors are called intrinsic semiconductors. At room temperature, some electrons from the valence band can acquire enough energy to cross the band gap and enter the conduction band. The number of conduction electrons is equal to the number of holes is an intrinsic semiconductor. The number of charge carriers can be changed by doping of a suitable impurity in a pure semiconductor. Such semiconductors are known as extrinsic semiconductors. These are of two types (n-type and p-type).
A p-n junction is the basic building block of semiconductor devices. Two important processes occur during formation of a p-n junction: diffusion and drift. A 'depletion layer' is formed in a p-n junction. This is responsible for a junction potential barrier. A semiconductor diode is basically a p-n junction with metallic contacts provided at the ends for the application of an external voltage. A diode can be forward-biased or reverse-biased. The barrier height and the depletion layer width in a p-n junction changes depending on the nature of the biasing.

    1. Which of the following statements is not true? (1)
      1. The resistance of an intrinsic semiconductor decreases with the increase of temperature.
      2. Doping pure Si with trivalent impurities gives p-type semiconductors.
      3. The majority charge carriers in n-type semiconductors are holes.
      4. A p-n junction can act as a semiconductor diode.

        OR

    2. In a unbiased p-n junction: (1)
      1. the diffusion current is zero everywhere.
      2. the drift current is zero everywhere.
      3. the electric potential is zero everywhere.
      4. the drift current and the diffusion current cancel each other.
  2. The impurity atoms with which pure Ge should be doped to convert it into an n-type semiconductor, is: (1)
    1. Boron
    2. Phosphorous
    3. Aluminium
    4. Indium
  3. The energy band gap in Ge at 0 K is about: (1)
    1. 0.72 eV
    2. {tex}1 \cdot 1 {eV}{/tex}
    3. 3.0 eV
    4. 5.4 eV
  4. In a p-n junction diode under reverse bias, the barrier height: (1)
    1. is reduced and the depletion layer width decreases.
    2. is reduced and the depletion layer width increases.
    3. increases and the depletion layer width also increases.
    4. increases and the depletion layer width decreases.
 4
30.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A compound microscope is an optical instrument used for observing highly magnified images of tiny objects. It consists of two convex lenses. The lens near the object is called the objective and the lens near the eye is called the eyepiece. The magnifying power of a compound microscope is given by {tex}m=m_0 m_e{/tex}, where {tex}m_0{/tex} is the magnification produced by objective lens and {tex}m_e{/tex} is the magnification produced by eyepiece. The expression for {tex}{m}_{{e}}{/tex} depends on whether the final image is formed at the near point or at infinity (normal adjustment).

    1. A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length {tex}f_o=2.0 {~cm}{/tex} and an eyepiece of focal length {tex}{f}_{{e}}=6.25 {~cm}{/tex} separated by a distance of 15 cm . How far should an object be placed from the objective so that the final image is formed at infinity? (1)
      1. 3.45 cm
      2. 5 cm
      3. 1.29 cm
      4. 2.59 cm

        OR

    2. A person with normal near point 25 cm, using a compound microscope with an objective of focal length 0.8 cm and eyepiece of focal length 2.5 cm can bring an object placed at 0.9 cm from the objective in sharp focus. If the image formed by the objective is at a distance of 2.27 cm from the eyepiece, the separation between the lenses is: (1)
      1. {tex} 7.47 {~cm}{/tex}
      2. 8.47 cm
      3. 9.47 cm
      4. {tex}10 .47 {~cm}{/tex}
  2. A compound microscope consists of an objective of focal length 2 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 10 cm. The eyepiece is kept 20 cm away from the objective. Find the magnifying power of the microscope if the final image is formed at infinity. (1)
    1. 7
    2. 10
    3. 14
    4. 16
  3. The intermediate image formed in a compound microscope is: (1)
    1. real, inverted and magnified
    2. real, erect and magnified
    3. virtual, erect and magnified
    4. virtual, inverted and magnified
  4. The focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece of a compound microscope are {tex}f_o{/tex} and {tex}f_e{/tex} respectively. Which of the following statements is correct, in normal adjustment? (1)
    1. The object is at a distance less than {tex}{f}_{{o}}{/tex} from the objective.
    2. The object is at a distance more than {tex}f_0{/tex} and less than {tex}2 f_0{/tex} from the objective.
    3. The intermediate image is formed at a distance slightly more than {tex}f_e{/tex} from the eyepiece.
    4. The intermediate image is formed at {tex}2 {f}_{{e}}{/tex} from the eyepiece.
 4

Section - E
31.(i)
  1. A parallel plate capacitor A of capacitance C is charged by a battery to a potential 'V'. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged identical capacitor B is connected across it. Calculate for the capacitor A the new value of the:
    1. charge
    2. potential difference
    3. energy stored
      Justify your answers.
  2. Draw the pattern of electric field lines due to:
    1. positively charged conducting sphere, and
    2. an electric dipole.
 5

OR
31.(ii)
  1. Write Coulomb's law of electrostatics in vector form. Apply it to determine the electric field at a point due to a system of point charges.
  2. {tex}{ABC}{/tex} is an equilateral triangle of side {tex}l{/tex}. Two point charges {tex}+2 \mu {C}{/tex} each, are located at points B and C. Find the sign and magnitude of the point charge {tex}q{/tex} to be kept at the midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of the side BC, so that the net electric field at point A becomes zero.
 5
32.(i)

A ray of light is incident on one face of a triangular glass prism of refractive index n and is refracted out from the opposite face. Deduce the expression for refractive index of glass prism in terms of the angle of minimum deviation and angle of the prism. Write the condition on {tex}n{/tex} for refraction to take place on the opposite face.

 5

OR
32.(ii)

State Huygens principle. Using it, draw a diagram and discuss the case of refraction of plane wave of light from a rarer medium to a denser medium at their plane interface. Hence derive Snell's law.

 5
33.(i)
  1. A series combination of {tex}{L}, {C}{/tex} and R is connected to an ac source {tex}{V}={V}_{{m}} \sin \omega{/tex}. Obtain:
    1. the impedance of the circuit using phasor diagram,
    2. the expression for the instantaneous current I, and
    3. the phase relationship of current to the applied voltage.
  2. Define power factor of an ac circuit. State the conditions under which it is:
    1. maximum,
    2. minimum.
 5

OR
33.(ii)
  1. Prove that the voltage is ahead of the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} rad in an ac circuit containing an ideal inductor.
  2. The currents through two inductors of self-inductance 12 mH and 24 mH are increasing with time at the same rate. Draw graphs showing the variation of the:
    1. magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor.
    2. energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
 5

Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity?
A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is kept at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d. Use Gauss' law to obtain the expression for the electric flux through the square.
If the point charge is now moved to a point at a distance '{tex}d{/tex}' from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux through the square will be affected.

Solution:

Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e.
{tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex}
Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity.
Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law.

Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as
{tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex}= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵo.
If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge.

These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets and if we draw a common tangent to all these spheres, we obtain the new position of the wavefront at a later time.

Discussion: In time {tex}\tau{/tex} distance BC travelled by the incident plane wavefront with the velocity {tex}v_1{/tex} is {tex}v_1 \tau{/tex}. To determine the shape of the refracted wave wavefront, draw a sphere of radius {tex}v_2 \tau(={AE}){/tex} from point A and draw a tangent from {tex}C{/tex} to {tex}E{/tex} as shown in the diagram.
From the above diagram
{tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A E C{/tex}
{tex} \sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{A C} {/tex}
and {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\nu_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex}
{tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(1)
As {tex}{n}_1=\frac{c}{v_1}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_2=\frac{c}{v_2}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{n_1}{n_2} {/tex} ...(2)
From equations (1) and (2)
{tex} n_1 \sin i=n_2 \sin r {/tex}

 5
33.(i)
  1. A series combination of {tex}{L}, {C}{/tex} and R is connected to an ac source {tex}{V}={V}_{{m}} \sin \omega{/tex}. Obtain:
    1. the impedance of the circuit using phasor diagram,
    2. the expression for the instantaneous current I, and
    3. the phase relationship of current to the applied voltage.
  2. Define power factor of an ac circuit. State the conditions under which it is:
    1. maximum,
    2. minimum.
Solution:
    1. Let resistance R, inductor L and capacitor C be connected in series with an alternate e.m.f.

      {tex} \varepsilon =\sqrt{I^2\left[R^2+\left(X_L-X_c\right)^2\right]} {/tex}
      {tex} Z =\frac{\varepsilon}{I} {/tex}
      {tex} Z =\sqrt{{R}^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2} {/tex}
    2. {tex} I_m =\frac{V_m}{Z} \sin (\omega t-\phi) {/tex}
    3. Phase angle {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{{V}_{{L}}-{V}_{{C}}}{{V}_{{R}}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P=E_V I_V \cos \phi{/tex} where {tex}{P}={/tex} true power and {tex}E_V I_V{/tex} is the apparent power. {tex}\phi{/tex} is phase difference between current and voltage.
    Power factor is defined as the ratio of true power to the apparent power.{tex} \text { Power factor }=\frac{\text { True Power }(P)}{\text { Apparent power }\left(E_V I_V\right)}=\cos \phi {/tex}
    1. Power factor is maximum when {tex}\phi=0{/tex} purely resistive circuit(It occurs when {tex}\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} at resonance in an LCR series circuit).
    2. Power factor is minimum when {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, in pure inductive or capacitive circuit.
 5

OR
33.(ii)
  1. Prove that the voltage is ahead of the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} rad in an ac circuit containing an ideal inductor.
  2. The currents through two inductors of self-inductance 12 mH and 24 mH are increasing with time at the same rate. Draw graphs showing the variation of the:
    1. magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor.
    2. energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
Solution:
  1. Let the voltage across the source be
    {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex}

    Using Kirchhoff's loop rule
    {tex} v-L \frac{d i}{d t}=0 {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{v}{L}=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} d i=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega {t} d t {/tex}
    Integrating
    {tex} i=\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} i=i_m \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) {/tex}
    This show that current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Voltage is ahead of current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex}
    1. Graph for induced e.m.f.
    2. Graph for energy stored

A point charge of 20 nC is fixed at the origin of {tex}x-y{/tex} plane. The work done in taking a second point charge of -50 nC from point ( {tex}4 \mathrm{~cm}, 0{/tex} ) to point {tex}(0,5 \mathrm{~cm}){/tex} is: a. {tex}45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} b. {tex}-45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} c. {tex}90 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} d. {tex}-90 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} Correct Answer: (a) {tex}45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} Explanation: {tex}45 \mu \mathrm{~J}{/tex} 1 2. In Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, the kinetic energy of an electron in {tex}{n}=2{/tex} orbit is: a. 1.7 eV b. {tex}3.4 {eV}{/tex} c. 6.8 eV d. {tex}10.2 {eV}{/tex} Correct Answer: (b) {tex}3.4 {eV}{/tex} Explanation: {tex}3.4 {eV}{/tex} 1 3. A galvanometer having a coil resistance of {tex}48 \Omega{/tex} shows full-scale deflection for a current of 2.0 A. It can be converted into an ammeter of range ( {tex}0-10 {~A}{/tex} ) by connecting: a. in series a resistance of {tex}8 \Omega{/tex} b. in parallel a resistance of {tex}8 \Omega{/tex} c. in parallel a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex} d. in series a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex} Correct Answer: (c) in parallel a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex} Explanation: in parallel a resistance of {tex}12 \Omega{/tex} 1 4. A charge Q is enclosed by a Gaussian spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is doubled, then the outward electric flux will a. increase four times b. be reduced to half c. remain the same d. be doubled Correct Answer: (c) remain the same Explanation: As the charge Q enclosed by the Gaussian surface remains the same, so {tex}\phi=\frac {q}{ \varepsilon_{0^{\prime}}}{/tex} will also remain the same. 1 5. A rectangular loop of area vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{A}}=[\hat{i}+\sqrt{3} \hat{j}] \times 10^{-2} {~m}^{2}{/tex} is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}=\left[4 .0 {t}^{2}+2 .0\right] \hat{{i}}{/tex} , where B is in tesla and t in seconds. At {tex}{t}=1 .0 {~s}{/tex} , the magnitude of induced emf in the loop is: a. 80 mV b. {tex}0 .11 {~V}{/tex} c. 0.22 V d. 0.8 V Correct Answer: (a) 80 mV Explanation: 80 mV 1 6. Which of the following electromagnetic waves are known as 'heat waves'? a. UV rays b. IR rays c. X-rays d. Gamma rays Correct Answer: (b) IR rays Explanation: IR rays 1 7. Which of the following assumptions has been used to obtain the relation {tex}\frac{v_{s}}{v_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} for a transformer? a. The resistance of the primary coil is large. b. The same flux links both the primary and the secondary coils. c. The resistance of secondary coil is large. d. The transformer is {tex}100 \%{/tex} efficient. Correct Answer: (b) The same flux links both the primary and the secondary coils. Explanation: The same flux links both the primary and the secondary coils. 1 8. The figure shows the variation of maximum kinetic energy ( {tex}{K}_{\max }{/tex} ) of emitted electrons as a function of the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of radiation incident on a photosensitive surface. The slope of the curve is: a. h b. {tex}\frac{{h}}{v}{/tex} c. {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} d. he Correct Answer: (a) h Explanation: h 1 9. The electric field due to an infinitely long, straight, uniformly and positively charged wire at a point at distance {tex}r{/tex} from the wire is: a. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed outward b. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} and is directed outward c. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed inward d. proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} and is directed inward Correct Answer: (a) proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed outward Explanation: proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{{r}}{/tex} and is directed outward 1 10. The potential energy of a pair of nucleons is minimum when they are separated by about: a. {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-15} {~m}{/tex} b. {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-10} {~m}{/tex} c. {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-13} {~m}{/tex} d. {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex} Correct Answer: (a) {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-15} {~m}{/tex} Explanation: {tex}0.8 \times 10^{-15} {~m}{/tex} 1 11. Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry the same current and attract each other with a force F per unit length. If the current is doubled in each conductor and their separation is made half, the force per unit length becomes: a. {tex}\frac{{F}}{2}{/tex} b. 2 F c. 4 F d. 8 F Correct Answer: (d) 8 F Explanation: 8 F 1 12. Whenever a magnet is moved either towards or away from a conducting coil, an emf is induced, the magnitude of which is independent of: a. the strength of the magnetic field b. the speed with which the magnet is moved c. the number of turns in the coil d. the resistance of the coil Correct Answer: (d) the resistance of the coil Explanation: Induced emf depends on the total flux {tex}\times{/tex} current per second, i.e., on the strength of the magnet, speed of moving the magnet, number of turns of the coil and area of cross-section of the coil. Only the current is decided by the emf and the resistance. {tex}\therefore{/tex} emf is independent of the resistance of the coil. 1 Questions number 13 to 16 are Assertion (A) and Reason (R) type questions. Two statements are given - one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below. Reason (R): If radiation falling on a photosensitive surface consists of different wavelengths, the energy of emitted photoelectrons by absorbing photons of different wavelengths, shall be different. a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. c. A is true but R is false. d. A is false but R is true. Correct Answer: (b) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. Explanation: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. 1 14. Assertion (A): The electrical conductivity of a pure Ge crystal increases with increase in its temperature. Reason (R): The number of electrons excited by thermal excitation from the valence band to the conduction band, in a semiconductor, increases with increase in temperature. a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. c. A is true but R is false. d. A is false but R is true. Correct Answer: (a) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. Explanation: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. 1 15. Assertion (A): Electrostatic force is a conservative force. Reason (R): In an electrostatic field, the work done between two points per unit positive charge depends on the path followed. a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. c. A is true but R is false. d. A is false but R is true. Correct Answer: (c) A is true but R is false. Explanation: A is true but R is false. 1 16. Assertion (A): The image of an object placed between {tex}f{/tex} and {tex}2 f{/tex} from a convex lens can be seen on a screen. If the screen is removed, image is not formed. Reason (R): Rays from a given point on the object placed between f and 2f, after passing through a convex lens, do not converge on a point in space. a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. c. A is true but R is false. d. Both A and R are false. Correct Answer: (d) Both A and R are false. Explanation: Both A and R are false. 1 Section - B 17. An electron of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}e{/tex} is accelerated from rest through a potential V. Prove that, after acceleration, the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is proportional to {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{~V}}}{/tex} . Solution: Momentum and kinetic energy are related as {tex}p=\sqrt{2 m K}{/tex} {tex}p=\sqrt{2 m e V}{/tex} De Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda =\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda =\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda \alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex} Circuit diagram for characteristics of a p-n junction diode 1 Describing the method to obtain p-n junction diode characteristics Drawing the shape of characteristics A very small current flows till the applied voltage reaches the barrier potential in the forward bias p-n junction diode. Diode current increases significantly when applied voltage becomes greater than barrier potential. 2 18. With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the method to obtain {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction diode characteristic when it is forward biased. Draw the shape of the characteristic. Solution: A very small current flows till the applied voltage reaches the barrier potential in the forward bias p-n junction diode. Diode current increases significantly when applied voltage becomes greater than barrier potential 2 19. (i) A plano-convex lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is placed coaxially in contact with a biconcave lens of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} as shown in the figure. All curved faces are of radius of curvature {tex}R{/tex} each. Obtain the expression for the focal length of the combined lens. Solution: {tex} \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) {/tex} For plano convex lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\left(\mu_1-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{\left(\mu_1-1\right)}{R} {/tex} For concave lens {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =\left(\mu_2-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-R}-\frac{1}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_2} =-2 \frac{\left(\mu_2-1\right)}{R} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f} =\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} {/tex} {tex} f =\frac{R}{\mu_1-2 \mu_2+1} {/tex} 2 OR (ii) A convex lens is kept coaxially on the left side of a concave mirror at a distance of 5f from it where f is focal length of each of them. An object is kept at a distance of 2f on the left side of the convex lens. Draw the ray diagram showing the formation of the image by the combination. Find the distance of the final image from the mirror. Solution: {tex} u=-3 f {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{-1}{f}=-\frac{1}{3 f}+\frac{1}{v} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{-3}{2} f {/tex} 2 20. The figure shows a network of resistors connected across a battery. Find the current supplied by the battery of emf 5 V and internal resistance {tex}\frac{2}{3} \Omega{/tex} . Solution: {tex} \frac{{R}_{{AB}}}{{R}_{{BC}}}=\frac{{R}_{{AD}}}{{R}_{{DC}}}=\frac{4}{1} {/tex} The bridge is balanced. Resistance of the circuit between points A and C. {tex} \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{(8+2)}+\frac{1}{(4+1)} {/tex} {tex} R=\frac{10}{3} \Omega {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{{E}}{{R}+{r}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{5}{\frac{10}{3}+\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} {I}=1.25 {~A} {/tex} 2 21. In a Young's double-slit experiment with light of wavelength 520 nm, the fringe width is 0.52 mm. What will be the fringe width if the entire apparatus is immersed in water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} )? Solution: {tex} \beta =\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\beta^{\prime}}{\beta} =\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{v / v}{c / v}=\frac{1}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\beta}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{0.52}{4 / 3} {/tex} {tex} =0.39 {~mm} {/tex} 2 Section - C 22. Define electric field. Write its SI unit. A point charge {tex}q=25 \mu {C}{/tex} is located at the point (0, 0) in x-y plane. Find (i) the magnitude of electric field at point ( {tex}3 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}{/tex} ), and (ii) the angle the field makes with the x-axis. Solution: Electric field: Electric field due to a point charge in space may be defined as the force that a unit positive charge would experience if placed at that point. SI unit of electric field is {tex}{NC}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}{Vm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =9 \times 10^7 {NC}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} \theta =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \approx 53^{\circ} {/tex} 3 23. (i) A {tex}20 \mu {~F}{/tex} capacitor is connected to a {tex}220 {~V}, \frac{125}{\pi} {~Hz}{/tex} ac source. Find the capacitive reactance and the peak value of current in the circuit. If the frequency of the ac source is doubled, by what factor will the capacitive reactance and the current be changed? Solution: {tex} X_c =\frac{1}{2 \pi v C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times \frac{125}{\pi} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} {/tex} {tex} =200 \Omega {/tex} {tex} I_0 =\frac{V_0}{X_C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{220 \times 1.414}{200} {/tex} {tex} =1.6 {~A} \text { or } 1.1 \sqrt{2} {~A} {/tex} {tex}{X}_{{c}}{/tex} becomes half Current becomes double. 3 OR (ii) An electron (mass {tex}9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}{/tex} and charge {tex}1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}{/tex} ) is moving in a circle with a speed of {tex}3.2 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}{/tex} in a magnetic field of {tex}4.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}{/tex} perpendicular to it. Calculate: the radius of the circular path, its frequency of rotation, and its energy. Solution: {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 10^7}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} = 0.4 {~m} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{3.2 \times 10^7}{2 \pi \times 0.4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4}{\pi} \times 10^7 {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} {E} =\frac{1}{2} {~m} v^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 3.2 \times 10^{14} {/tex} {tex} =4.608 \times 10^{-16} {~J} {/tex} 3 24. Define binding energy (BE) of a nucleus. An unstable nucleus has mass number {tex}{A}=240{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{BE}}{{A}}=7 \cdot 6 {MeV}{/tex} . It splits in two fragments, each of {tex}{A}=120{/tex} with {tex}\frac{{BE}}{{A}}=8.5 {MeV}{/tex} . Calculate the energy released. Solution: Binding energy: Energy released in bringing all the nucleons of a nucleus together to form a nucleus. Alternatively- Energy required to separate the nucleons from the nucleus. Binding Energy of X, B.E x {tex}=240 \times 7.6{/tex} {tex} =1824 {MeV} {/tex} Binding Energy of Y, {tex} \text { B. } {E}_{{Y}}=120 \times 8.5=1020 {MeV} {/tex} Energy released {tex}{E}=2\left({~B} \cdot {E}_{{Y}}\right)-\left({B} \cdot {E}_{{X}}\right){/tex} {tex} =(2 \times 1020)-1824 {/tex} {tex} =216 {MeV} {/tex} 3 25. Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity? A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is kept at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d. Use Gauss' law to obtain the expression for the electric flux through the square. If the point charge is now moved to a point at a distance ' {tex}d{/tex} ' from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux through the square will be affected. Solution: Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e. {tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity. Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law. Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as {tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵ o . If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge. 3 26. What is thermonuclear fusion? Calculate the energy released in MeV in the fusion reaction given below: {tex}{ }_{1}^{2} {H}+{ }_{1}^{3} {H} \longrightarrow{ }_{2}^{4} {He}+{n}{/tex} Given: {tex}{m}\left({ }_{1}^{2} {H}\right)=2 \cdot 014102 {u}, {m}\left({ }_{1}^{3} {H}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \cdot 016049 {u}, {m}\left({ }_{2}^{4} {He}\right)=4 \cdot 002603 {u}{/tex} , {tex}{m}_{{n}}=1 \cdot 008665 {u}, 1 {u}=930 {MeV} / {c}^{2}{/tex} Solution: Thermonuclear fusion: When two light nuclei fuse to form a larger nucleus, energy is released Mass defect {tex} \Delta m =\left[m\left({ }_1^2 H\right)+m\left({ }_1^3 H\right)\right]-\left[m\left({ }_2^4 H e\right)+m_n\right] {/tex} {tex} =(2.014102+3.016049)-(4.002603+1.008665) {/tex} {tex} \Delta m =0.018883 {u} {/tex} Energy released {tex} {E}= \Delta m \times 930 {MeV} {/tex} {tex} =0.018883 \times 930 {/tex} {tex} =17.56 {MeV} {/tex} 3 27.
